description: A word-level particle that creates comparative forms of adjectives, enabling gradable qualities to be measured against standards. More than ranking, 'mo' acknowledges the relational nature of qualities—that 'tall' or 'kind' only gain meaning through comparison, whether explicit or implied.
sound_symbolism: The particle begins with 'm', the labial nasal that hums with measurement—lips coming together as if weighing one thing against another. This measuring consonant meets 'o', the round vowel of wholeness that keeps comparison from becoming division. Together, 'mo' sounds like comparison itself: the 'm' evaluates while the 'o' maintains the integrity of what is compared. The nasal resonance makes comparison felt rather than merely calculated, keeping it human rather than mechanical.
grammatical_notes: Occupies Slot 2, appearing immediately before the adjective it modifies (whether in attributive or adverbial function). Creates regular comparatives without suppletion—'mo welao' (better, more good) rather than an irregular form. The standard of comparison uses the preposition 'sheo' (than), and the sheo-phrase precedes the comparative predicate: 'thepalu sheo shelira mo phelora nai' (the garden is more beautiful than the forest). Combines with the focus particle to form the superlative: 'mo ko phelora' (most beautiful). Context determines whether comparison implies superiority or simply difference—'mo' itself remains evaluatively neutral.

pillars.solarpunk-values: Measuring progress requires comparison—more renewable energy, less waste, better soil health. 'Mo' enables assessment of improvement without perfectionism, tracking movement toward regenerative goals while acknowledging the journey continues.
pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: The particle embodies the teaching on the constructed nature of qualities—nothing is inherently tall or short, fast or slow, but only in relation. 'Mo' grammaticalizes this wisdom that comparison reveals the emptiness of absolute qualities.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: By using soft nasal sounds rather than harsh stops, 'mo' enables comparison without competition. It allows us to note differences without creating hierarchy, to observe relations without establishing dominance.
